location: Tokyo National Museum (東京国立博物館)
date_notes: Dating is based on paleographic analysis of the Siddham script. The manuscript may have been brought to Japan during the Nara period (710-794 CE), possibly by a returning Japanese monk or a visiting Chinese/Indian monk.

physical_description.folios: Single folio, written on both sides
physical_description.condition: Well-preserved, some areas darkened with age
physical_description.script_style: North Indian Siddham, characteristic of manuscripts from the 7th-8th centuries

notes: The Hōryūji manuscript is one of the oldest and most important Sanskrit witnesses for the Heart Sutra. It was one of Conze's primary sources for his critical edition (siglum 'Ja'). The manuscript preserves the short recension without frame narrative. It lacks the oṃ before 'gate gate' and lacks the elaborate maṅgala found in later Nepalese manuscripts, supporting the view that these are late additions.
text_iast:
namas sarvajñāya |

āryāvalokiteśvaro bodhisatvo gambhīrāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caryāṃ caramāṇo vyavalokayati sma | pañcaskandhās tāṃś ca svabhāvaśūnyān paśyati sma |

iha śāriputra rūpaṃ śūnyatā śūnyataiva rūpam | rūpān na pṛthak śūnyatā śūnyatāyā na pṛthag rūpam | yad rūpaṃ sā śūnyatā yā śūnyatā tad rūpam | evam eva vedanāsaṃjñāsaṃskāravijñānāni |

iha śāriputra sarvadharmāḥ śūnyatālakṣaṇā anutpannā aniruddhā amalā avimalā anūnā aparipūrṇāḥ |

tasmāc chāriputra śūnyatāyāṃ na rūpaṃ na vedanā na saṃjñā na saṃskārā na vijñānam | na cakṣuḥśrotraghrāṇajihvākāyamanāṃsi | na rūpaśabdagandharasaspraṣṭavyadharmāḥ | na cakṣurdhātur yāvan na manovijñānadhātuḥ | nāvidyā nāvidyākṣayo yāvan na jarāmaraṇaṃ na jarāmaraṇakṣayaḥ | na duḥkhasamudayanirodhamārgā | na jñānaṃ na prāptiḥ |

tasmāc chāriputra aprāptitvena bodhisatvasya prajñāpāramitām āśritya viharatyacittāvaraṇaḥ | cittāvaraṇanāstitvād atrasto viparyāsātikrānto niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ |

tryadhvavyavasthitāḥ sarvabuddhāḥ prajñāpāramitām āśritya anuttarāṃ samyaksaṃbodhim abhisaṃbuddhāḥ |

tasmāj jñātavyaṃ prajñāpāramitā mahāmantro mahāvidyāmantro 'nuttaramantro samasamamantraḥ sarvaduḥkhapraśamanaḥ satyam amithyatvāt |

prajñāpāramitāyām ukto mantraḥ | tadyathā gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā |

iti prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtraṃ samāptam |
text_siddham: [Siddham script text would be represented here - requires specialized Unicode support]

segments.notes: Simple salutation to sarvajña (omniscience/the omniscient one), unlike the elaborate Tantric maṅgala in later Nepalese manuscripts. This is the more historically appropriate salutation (Attwood 2024).

segments.iast: āryāvalokiteśvaro bodhisatvo gambhīrāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caryāṃ caramāṇo vyavalokayati sma | pañcaskandhās tāṃś ca svabhāvaśūnyān paśyati sma |
notes: Uses bodhisatva (single t) - proper Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit spelling
pañcaskandhās appears to be nominative here (as in Conze), though some read accusative pañcaskandhāṃs
gambhīrāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caryāṃ - locative construction differs slightly from Conze's gambhīrāṃ prajñāpāramitācaryāṃ
variants_from_conze: gambhīrāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caryāṃ vs Conze gambhīrāṃ prajñāpāramitācaryāṃ

segments.iast: iha śāriputra rūpaṃ śūnyatā śūnyataiva rūpam | rūpān na pṛthak śūnyatā śūnyatāyā na pṛthag rūpam | yad rūpaṃ sā śūnyatā yā śūnyatā tad rūpam | evam eva vedanāsaṃjñāsaṃskāravijñānāni |
segments.notes: Includes 'yad rūpaṃ sā śūnyatā' line which Nattier (1992) identified as absent from Chinese and likely a later interpolation. Hōryūji includes it, suggesting this line was already in some Sanskrit traditions by the 7th-8th century.

segments.iast: iha śāriputra sarvadharmāḥ śūnyatālakṣaṇā anutpannā aniruddhā amalā avimalā anūnā aparipūrṇāḥ |

tasmāc chāriputra śūnyatāyāṃ na rūpaṃ na vedanā na saṃjñā na saṃskārā na vijñānam | na cakṣuḥśrotraghrāṇajihvākāyamanāṃsi | na rūpaśabdagandharasaspraṣṭavyadharmāḥ | na cakṣurdhātur yāvan na manovijñānadhātuḥ | nāvidyā nāvidyākṣayo yāvan na jarāmaraṇaṃ na jarāmaraṇakṣayaḥ | na duḥkhasamudayanirodhamārgā | na jñānaṃ na prāptiḥ |
notes: Uses kṣaya (destruction) not nirodha (cessation) - evidence of back-translation from Chinese (Nattier 1992)
Ends with 'na jñānaṃ na prāptiḥ' - does NOT include na-aprāptiḥ
Absence of na-aprāptiḥ supports Huifeng (2014) that this is a later interpolation

significant_readings.ksaya_vs_nirodha: Uses kṣaya (jarāmaraṇakṣayaḥ) matching Chinese 盡 (jìn), not standard Prajñāpāramitā nirodha
significant_readings.no_aprapti: Lacks na-aprāptiḥ that appears in Conze's edition and later manuscripts

segments.iast: tasmāc chāriputra aprāptitvena bodhisatvasya prajñāpāramitām āśritya viharatyacittāvaraṇaḥ | cittāvaraṇanāstitvād atrasto viparyāsātikrānto niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ |
notes: bodhisatvasya (genitive) - Conze noted variation between genitive and nominative across witnesses
niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ WITHOUT prāptaḥ - masculine nominative adjective describing bodhisattva
This supports Attwood's (2018a) analysis that niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ is a bahuvrīhi compound

significant_readings.nistha_nirvana: niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ (without prāptaḥ) - bahuvrīhi 'one whose nirvāṇa is complete'

segments.iast: tryadhvavyavasthitāḥ sarvabuddhāḥ prajñāpāramitām āśritya anuttarāṃ samyaksaṃbodhim abhisaṃbuddhāḥ |
tasmāj jñātavyaṃ prajñāpāramitā mahāmantro mahāvidyāmantro 'nuttaramantro samasamamantraḥ sarvaduḥkhapraśamanaḥ satyam amithyatvāt |
segments.notes: Uses samasamamantraḥ not 'samasama-mantraḥ - slight orthographic difference

segments.iast: prajñāpāramitāyām ukto mantraḥ | tadyathā gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā |
notes: This is significant: Conze (1967) includes oṃ but notes it is absent from most witnesses
Supports Attwood's (2021) argument that oṃ is a late Tantric interpolation

significant_readings.no_om: Mantra begins directly with 'gate gate' without oṃ prefix

segments.notes: Simple colophon: 'Thus the Heart Sūtra of the Perfection of Wisdom is complete.'

for_text_criticism: One of the oldest Sanskrit manuscript witnesses
Preserves readings that differ from Conze's conflated text
Absence of oṃ in mantra confirms it as late interpolation
Absence of na-aprāptiḥ supports Huifeng's analysis
niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ without prāptaḥ supports Attwood's bahuvrīhi analysis
Uses simple namas sarvajñāya maṅgala, not elaborate Tantric salutation
for_dating: 7th-8th century date makes it roughly contemporary with Chinese composition (ca. 649-661 CE)
Demonstrates Sanskrit version existed within a generation or two of Chinese original
Supports Nattier's thesis that Sanskrit is back-translation
critical_significance.conze_usage: Conze designated this manuscript 'Ja' and treated it as authoritative, though his critical edition sometimes departs from its readings in favor of later Nepalese manuscripts

diplomatic_editions.editor: Müller, F. Max and Nanjio, Bunyiu
diplomatic_editions.title: The Ancient Palm Leaves containing the Prajñāpāramitā-Hṛidaya Sūtra and Uṣṇīṣa-vijaya-Dhāraṇī
diplomatic_editions.publication: Buddhist Texts from Japan, Vol. 1, Part 3
diplomatic_editions.notes: First diplomatic edition; introduced punctuation not in original manuscript

diplomatic_editions.title: An Annotated Translation of The Prajñāpāramitāhṛdaya
diplomatic_editions.publication: Association of Esoteric Buddhist Studies, No. 209

Attwood, Jayarava (2018a). 'A Note on Niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ in the Heart Sutra.' JOCBS 14: 10-17.
Attwood, Jayarava (2024). 'Revised Editions of the Prajñāpāramitāhṛdaya and Bānrěbōluómìduō xīn jīng.' Asian Literature and Translation 11(1): 1-42.
Huifeng (2014). 'Apocryphal Treatment for Conze's Heart Problems.' JOCBS 6: 72-105.
